section.title: The Oracle against the Philistines
section.sectionRationale: The second panel in the oracles against the nations turns from Egypt to Philistia, Judah's coastal neighbor and ancient enemy. A flood from the north sweeps away Gaza, Ashkelon, and the remnant of Caphtor, and the chapter ends by asking the one question that matters: how long until the sword of the LORD can rest, and the answer is, not until His purpose is finished.
section.thesis: The sword of the LORD moves against the Philistines because He has appointed it, and it cannot rest until His commanded work is done.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the word against the Philistines before Gaza's fall; the flood rising from the north; the day appointed to destroy the Philistines; and the sword of the LORD, unable to rest until it finishes its errand.

units.label: The word against the Philistines before Gaza's fall

verses.text: This is the word of the LORD that came to Jeremiah the prophet about the Philistines before Pharaoh struck down Gaza.

units.label: The flood rising from the north

verses.text: This is what the LORD says: “See how the waters are rising from the north and becoming an overflowing torrent. They will overflow the land and its fullness, the cities and their inhabitants. The people will cry out, and all who dwell in the land will wail
at the sound of the galloping hooves of stallions, the rumbling of chariots, and the clatter of their wheels. The fathers will not turn back for their sons; their hands will hang limp.

units.label: The day appointed to destroy the Philistines

verses.text: For the day has come to destroy all the Philistines, to cut off from Tyre and Sidon every remaining ally. Indeed, the LORD is about to destroy the Philistines, the remnant from the coasts of Caphtor.
The people of Gaza will shave their heads in mourning; Ashkelon will be silenced. O remnant of their valley, how long will you gash yourself?

units.label: The sword of the LORD cannot rest

verses.text: ‘Alas, O {{chereb|sword}} of the LORD, how long until you rest? Return to your {{taar|sheath}}; cease and be still!’
How can it rest when the LORD has commanded it? He has appointed it against Ashkelon and the shore of its coastland.”

chereb.gloss: Sword. Here personified and even addressed directly, 'O sword of the LORD', as though it were a living agent of divine justice that cannot simply be told to stop.

taar.gloss: Sheath or scabbard, the same word used elsewhere for a razor or blade case. The plea to return the sword to its sheath pictures a desperate wish for judgment to cease, answered immediately by the reminder that the LORD Himself has commanded and appointed it.

basic.context: This short oracle turns from Egypt to Philistia, the coastal enemy that had harassed Israel since the days of the judges and the era of Samson, Saul, and David. Dated loosely to an Egyptian campaign against Gaza, it pictures an invading flood from the north sweeping the land, silencing the great Philistine cities of Gaza and Ashkelon, and ends with an almost pleading question: how long until the sword can rest, answered by the sober truth that it cannot, because the LORD Himself has sent it.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real historical oracle against a real ancient people, substantially fulfilled in Babylon's westward campaigns that devastated the Philistine coastal cities in the early sixth century BC. It is not a timeless verdict on any modern nation bearing a similar name; the enduring truth is that the LORD directs even the most terrifying invasions toward His own appointed purposes, and no one, not even the sword itself, can call a halt before He is finished.
basic.originalLanguages: The chereb, sword, of verse 6 is addressed directly, as though pleading with it to return to its ta'ar, its sheath, and rest. The poignant personification only deepens the point of verse 7: the sword cannot obey that plea, because it obeys a higher command, the LORD who appointed it against Ashkelon and her coastland.
basic.typology: The sword commanded and unable to rest until its errand is finished anticipates the certainty of final judgment in Christ's hands, an assignment that likewise cannot be recalled or turned aside once given, Revelation 19:15.
basic.christ: The sword of the LORD cannot rest until it has done what it was sent to do, verse 7, a picture of divine justice too resolute to be talked down. That same certainty belongs to Christ, who alone will execute the last judgment precisely as appointed, neither early nor late, and who alone, unlike this sword, can also sheath wrath forever for those who take refuge in Him, having already borne its edge at the cross.
basic.god: God is sovereign even over the timing of judgment: an invading flood, a sword unable to rest, all move exactly on His schedule and toward His purpose, not by chance and not beyond His command. His government of history reaches every coastline, not only Israel's own land.
basic.crossReferences: Amos 1:6-8 pronounces a related judgment on the Philistine cities. Zephaniah 2:4-7 promises the coastland will one day belong to the remnant of Judah. Ezekiel 25:15-17 gives a parallel oracle against Philistia. Revelation 19:15 pictures Christ's own sword of final judgment.
basic.oneStory: This brief oracle shows a sword that cannot rest until the LORD's appointed work is finished, a certainty of judgment that runs all the way to Christ, who alone executes the last judgment exactly as commanded, and who alone can turn that same sword's wrath aside for all who are found in Him.

context.literary: The second of six panels in the oracles against the nations, chapters 46 to 51, notably brief and built almost entirely from flood and sword imagery, closing with a rare moment of direct address to the sword itself.
context.historical: Dated loosely to before Pharaoh struck down Gaza, likely referring to an Egyptian campaign in the late seventh century BC; the oracle's real fulfillment came through Babylon's later campaigns along the Philistine coast, which effectively ended Philistia as a distinct people by the early sixth century BC.
context.canonical: Philistia appears repeatedly among the nations judged by Israel's prophets, Amos 1:6-8, Zephaniah 2:4-7, and Ezekiel 25:15-17, showing a settled prophetic conviction that the LORD's justice was never confined to Israel alone.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To announce that the LORD, not Egypt or Babylon, directs the invading flood against Philistia, and that His appointed judgment cannot be halted by anyone, however desperately its coming might be wished away.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes a specific historical judgment on the Philistine coast; it does not prescribe how believers should regard any people group today. The abiding principle is that divine justice, once commanded, runs its full course.

debates.question: Who is speaking the plea in verse 6, 'O sword of the LORD, how long until you rest'?

views.label: The prophet's own lament
views.case: Jeremiah, who elsewhere weeps over the judgments he must announce, voices a very human wish that the bloodshed would end, only to answer his own plea with the sober truth of verse 7.

views.label: A voice representing all who suffer the invasion
views.case: The cry gives words to the Philistines, or to any onlooker, begging an end to the violence, which the prophet then answers on the LORD's behalf: the sword serves a purpose greater than anyone's wish for it to stop.

debates.bearing: Either reading underlines the same truth: however earnestly anyone might wish divine judgment to pause, it proceeds until the LORD's purpose in sending it is accomplished.

study.originalLanguages: The chereb, sword, is personified in verse 6, pleaded with as though it could choose to return to its ta'ar, its sheath, and rest. Verse 7 answers that plea with the reason the sword cannot comply: the LORD Himself has commanded and appointed it, so no wish, however sincere, can recall it before its work is finished.

typology.type: The sword commanded, unable to rest until its errand is finished
typology.fulfillment: Christ's own certain and unstoppable execution of the final judgment, Revelation 19:15.

study.christ: The sword of the LORD cannot rest until it has done what it was sent to do, verse 7, a picture of divine justice too resolute to be talked down. That same certainty belongs to Christ, who alone will execute the last judgment precisely as appointed, neither early nor late, and who alone, unlike this sword, can also sheath wrath forever for those who take refuge in Him, having already borne its edge at the cross.
study.god: God is sovereign even over the timing of judgment: an invading flood, a sword unable to rest, all move exactly on His schedule and toward His purpose, not by chance and not beyond His command. His government of history reaches every coastline, not only Israel's own land.

crossReferences.note: A related judgment pronounced on the Philistine cities for their cruelty.
Promises that the Philistine coastland will one day belong to the remnant of Judah.
A parallel oracle against Philistia among Ezekiel's oracles against the nations.
Christ's own sword of final judgment, the ultimate answer to the question of when the sword can rest.

study.oneStory: This brief oracle shows a sword that cannot rest until the LORD's appointed work is finished, a certainty of judgment that runs all the way to Christ, who alone executes the last judgment exactly as commanded, and who alone can turn that same sword's wrath aside for all who are found in Him.

quiz.q: What image opens this oracle to describe the coming invasion, verse 2?

options.text: Waters rising from the north into an overflowing torrent.
options.note: Verse 2 pictures the invasion as a flood sweeping over the land and its people.

options.text: A fire falling from heaven.
options.note: This image belongs to other oracles, not this one.

options.text: A swarm of locusts.
options.note: A common image elsewhere in these oracles, but this chapter specifically pictures rising floodwaters.

options.text: A gentle rain.
options.note: The opposite; the water here is an overwhelming, destructive torrent.

quiz.q: Which Philistine cities are named as silenced by this judgment?

options.text: Gaza and Ashkelon.
options.note: Verse 5 names Gaza's mourning and Ashkelon's silence directly.

options.text: Nineveh and Babylon.
options.note: These are Mesopotamian cities, not Philistine ones, and are judged in other oracles.

options.text: Jerusalem and Bethlehem.
options.note: These are cities of Judah, not the Philistine coast addressed here.

options.text: Tyre and Sidon.
options.note: These Phoenician cities are mentioned as losing their Philistine ally in verse 4, but they are not the cities said to be silenced.

quiz.q: In verse 6, what does the voice plead with the sword of the LORD to do?

options.text: Return to its sheath and be still.
options.note: Verse 6 pictures a desperate wish for the sword to cease and rest.

options.text: Strike harder and faster.
options.note: The opposite of the plea in this verse.

options.text: Turn against Babylon instead.
options.note: Not the content of this plea, which asks simply for the violence to end.

options.text: Reveal who commanded it.
options.note: Verse 7 does reveal this, but the plea itself in verse 6 is simply for the sword to rest.

quiz.q: Why can the sword of the LORD not rest, according to verse 7?

options.text: Because the LORD Himself has commanded and appointed it against Ashkelon and the coastland.
options.note: Verse 7 states this directly as the reason the sword cannot simply stop.

options.text: Because it has a mind of its own, apart from God.
options.note: The text places the sword entirely under the LORD's command, not acting independently.

options.text: Because Egypt refuses to withdraw its army.
options.note: Egypt's campaign sets the historical occasion, but the reason given is the LORD's own command.

options.text: Because the Philistines refuse to surrender.
options.note: Plausible in a general sense, but the text specifically grounds the sword's unrest in the LORD's own appointment.

quiz.q: How does the unstoppable sword of this chapter point forward to Christ?

options.text: It anticipates the certain and unstoppable final judgment Christ will execute at His return.
options.note: Revelation 19:15 pictures Christ's own sword of final judgment, carried out exactly as appointed.

options.text: It shows that Christ has nothing to do with judgment at all.
options.note: Scripture consistently entrusts final judgment to Christ, not apart from Him.

options.text: It suggests judgment is random and unpredictable.
options.note: The opposite is true; this sword moves by direct divine command, not by chance.

options.text: It shows only that ancient warfare was violent.
options.note: True on the surface, but the passage's deeper point is the LORD's sovereign command over the timing of judgment.

questions: The invasion is pictured as a flood no one can hold back. What in your own life feels like rising water you cannot control, and where might God be at work in it?

The prophet, or perhaps the Philistines themselves, plead for the sword to rest, yet it cannot until the LORD's purpose is finished. Where are you asking God to stop something before His work in it is complete?
This chapter reminds Judah's neighbors that no coastline lies outside the LORD's justice. Where do you need reminding that God's rule is not confined to your own concerns?

The sword's certainty and Christ's final judgment are drawn together in Scripture. How does knowing that Christ has already borne judgment's edge for you change how you face the coming day of judgment?

translationNotes.note: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Jeremiah 47:1-7; no em dashes or line breaks required adjustment in this short chapter.
